learningGoal: How to spot simple opposite pairs quickly — you'll be a pro at this!
How to use the flip test — if the meaning reverses completely, it's an antonym (a word that means the opposite). This trick works every time!

screens.title: What's the opposite of "hot"?
screens.body:
Daisy is matching words with opposite meanings.

Did you know that almost every word has an exact mirror image — a word that means the complete opposite? **"hot"** means having a high temperature.

Can you spot which word flips it around completely? Careful — the 11+ loves sneaking in words that are close but not quite right!

screens.title: The Flip Test
screens.body: Here's a brilliant trick you'll use again and again! Put **"hot"** in a sentence, then swap it for **"cold"** — if the meaning **completely flips**, you've found the antonym. It's like pressing a reverse button!

interaction.sentence: An antonym is a word that means the ____ of another word
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Antonyms are words with opposite meanings — you've got this! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Antonyms mean the OPPOSITE of each other — think of them as mirror images.

steps.text: Flip it: The soup was **hot** → flip it → The soup was **cold**

steps.why: 'Warm' is a LESSER version of hot — it's on the same side, not the opposite side

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which word means the **opposite** of **"dark"**?

interaction.question: Which word is an antonym of "dark"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Dark' means having little light and 'bright' means having lots of light — they are exact opposites. 'Dim' is a lesser version of dark, not the opposite. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "bright". 'Dark' means having little light and 'bright' means having lots of light — they are exact opposites. 'Dim' is a lesser version of dark, not the opposite. ✓

screens.title: The Flip Test — your secret weapon!
screens.body: You've got a proper superpower now! Next time you need to find an antonym, just follow these three steps:

steps.text: 1. Define the word in your own words
2. Flip the meaning — what's the exact opposite?
steps.why: High → low, fast → slow, big → small

steps.text: 3. Watch out for RELATED words that aren't opposites

learningGoal: Why picking a DIFFERENT word isn't the same as picking an OPPOSITE — this catches loads of people out!
How to dodge the sneaky 'wrong direction' trap

screens.title: Is "damp" really the opposite of "dry"?
screens.body:
Evie is checking her friend's VR homework. The answer given was **"damp"** as the opposite of **"dry"** — because dry things aren't damp.

Hmm, that sounds reasonable at first... but is it actually right? Let's play detective and find out!

screens.title: Different is NOT the same as Opposite!
screens.body:
'Damp' means slightly moist — it's not the full opposite of 'dry'

This is one of the trickiest traps in the 11+! An antonym must **flip the meaning completely**. Words that are just DIFFERENT or RELATED don't count — they must be on the exact opposite end.

steps.why: 'Damp' means slightly moist — it's not the full opposite of 'dry'
'Wet' is the true opposite of 'dry'. 'Damp' is only slightly moist — it's halfway, not the full flip.

screens.title: Find the TRUE opposite!
screens.body: Which word is the **exact opposite** of **"hard"**?

interaction.question: Which is the true antonym of "hard"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Soft' is the true opposite of 'hard'. Both describe the same quality — firmness. 'Gentle' describes behaviour, not texture. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "soft". 'Soft' is the true opposite of 'hard'. Both describe the same quality — firmness. 'Gentle' describes behaviour, not texture.

screens.title: Opposite, not just different — remember this!
screens.body: Fantastic work! You're building a really useful skill here. Keep this in mind and you'll dodge a trap that catches loads of people:

steps.text: An antonym reverses the meaning entirely
steps.why: Not just related — the exact reverse

steps.text: Check the SAME quality is being flipped
steps.why: Width ↔ width, sound ↔ sound, price ↔ price

steps.text: If it's just DIFFERENT, it's NOT an antonym

subConcepts.name: Prefix Antonyms (un-, dis-, im-, in-, ir-)

learningGoal: How to use prefixes like un-, dis-, im-, in- or ir- to create an opposite — it's like having a magic switch!
How to choose which prefix goes with which word (there's a pattern, and once you spot it, you'll never forget)

screens.title: What happens when you add "un-" to "happy"?
screens.body:
Daisy is learning how prefixes create opposites.

Did you know you can turn a word into its opposite just by sticking a tiny **prefix** (a few letters) on the front? It's like having a magic undo button for words!

**"happy"** means feeling pleased and content. What happens when you stick **"un-"** on the front?

screens.title: The "un-" prefix
screens.body: The prefix **"un-"** means "not" or "the opposite of". It flips the meaning!

steps.text: More examples: kind → unkind, fair → unfair, lucky → unlucky

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which word is the **opposite** of **"tidy"**? Think about which prefix to use!

interaction.question: Which word is an antonym of "tidy"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Adding 'un-' to 'tidy' creates 'untidy' — the direct prefix opposite. 'Messy' means the same thing but isn't formed from the word 'tidy'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "untidy". Adding 'un-' to 'tidy' creates 'untidy' — the direct prefix opposite. 'Messy' means the same thing but isn't formed from the word 'tidy'. ✓

screens.title: Prefix power — your toolkit!
screens.body: You've just unlocked a proper superpower! Once you know these prefixes, you can crack loads of antonym questions in seconds. They all mean "not" or "opposite":

steps.why: im- before b/m/p, ir- before r ✓

learningGoal: How to choose which prefix matches which word in tricky cases — even grown-ups get these muddled!
Why you can't just stick any prefix on any word (English has some sneaky rules here)

screens.title: Why not "unvisible"?
screens.body:
Evie is wondering why we say 'impossible' not 'unpossible'.

The opposite of **"visible"** is **"invisible"** — but why do we use **"in-"** and not **"un-"**?

Believe it or not, English has secret rules about which prefix goes with which word — and you're about to crack the code!

screens.title: Matching the right prefix
screens.body: Words starting with 'v' usually take 'in-'. Here's the good news — there's a pattern, and once you know it, these questions become much easier!

steps.text: "unvisible" is NOT a real English word
steps.why: Using the wrong prefix creates a non-word!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which is the correct opposite of **"mature"**? Watch out for wrong prefixes!

interaction.question: Which is the correct antonym of "mature"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Mature' takes the prefix 'im-' because it starts with 'm'. We don't say 'unmature' — 'immature' is the correct form. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "immature". 'Mature' takes the prefix 'im-' because it starts with 'm'. We don't say 'unmature' — 'immature' is the correct form. ✓

screens.title: Prefix matching rules — your cheat sheet!
screens.body: Well done! Here's a handy cheat sheet you can keep in your head — knowing this puts you ahead of most people:

steps.text: im- before b, m, p: impossible, immature, imbalance
steps.why: The 'm' in 'im-' matches these starting sounds

steps.text: ir- before r: irregular, irresponsible, irrelevant
in- or dis- for most others: invisible, dishonest
steps.why: Learn the common ones and you'll spot them in tests ✓

learningGoal: How to find opposites for action words (verbs) — imagine pressing rewind!
Why picturing the REVERSE action helps you find the match every time

screens.title: What's the reverse of "expand"?
screens.body:
Isla is matching opposite action words.

**"expand"** is an action word (a verb). It means: to grow larger or spread out.

Here's a really fun way to think about it: imagine watching the action on video and pressing **rewind** — what word describes what you'd see playing backwards?

screens.title: Reverse the action!
screens.body: Think about **"expand"** — it means to grow larger or spread out. Now imagine you're watching a video of that action and you press REWIND. What does the reversed action look like? That's your antonym!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Picture, reverse, then match. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by reading the word, then picture it, reverse it, and match.

steps.text: "expand" = to grow larger or spread out
Now REVERSE it: to shrink or become smaller
steps.why: What does the opposite look like?
The balloon will **expand** → reverse → The balloon will **contract**

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which word shows the **opposite action** to **"create"**?

interaction.question: Which is an antonym of "create"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Create' means to bring into existence and 'destroy' means to put an end to existence — they are exact opposite actions. 'Break' means to damage, not to completely undo creation. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "destroy". 'Create' means to bring into existence and 'destroy' means to put an end to existence — they are exact opposite actions. 'Break' means to damage, not to completely undo creation. ✓

visualProps.relationship: to make or bring something into existence
visualProps.label: Find the REVERSE action

screens.title: Reverse the action — nailed it!
screens.body: You've picked up a brilliant strategy here. The rewind trick works for any action word antonym:

steps.why: Up → down, in → out, grow → shrink

steps.text: 3. Pick the word that matches the reversed picture

learningGoal: How to tell the difference between graded antonyms (opposites with levels in between, like hot-warm-cool-cold) and complementary antonyms (opposites with nothing in between, like alive/dead) — this is fascinating stuff!
Why graded antonyms have 'in-between' steps but complementary ones don't — once you see it, you can't unsee it

screens.title: "hot" and "cold" — what type of opposite?
screens.body:
Daisy is discovering that some opposites have a scale.

Did you know there are actually **two types** of opposite? Most people have no idea about this — so knowing it gives you a real edge!

• **Graded** — opposites with steps in between (hot → warm → cool → cold)
• **Complementary** — either/or with NO middle (alive or dead)

Which type are **"hot"** and **"cold"**?

screens.title: Two types of opposite
screens.body: 'Hot' and 'cold' are GRADED antonyms — there are steps in between (warm, cool). You can be a bit hot or very hot.

steps.why: 'Hot' and 'cold' are GRADED antonyms — there are steps in between (warm, cool). You can be a bit hot or very hot.

steps.text: Scale: boiling → hot → warm → cool → cold → freezing
steps.why: See all the steps in between!
You can't be 'a bit alive' — you're either alive or dead!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Are **"happy"** and **"sad"** graded or complementary antonyms?

Think: is there anything in between?

interaction.question: What type of antonym pair is "happy" and "sad"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Happy' and 'sad' are graded antonyms — there are levels in between like content, pleased, disappointed. You can be a bit happy or very happy. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "graded". 'Happy' and 'sad' are graded antonyms — there are levels in between like content, pleased, disappointed. You can be a bit happy or very happy. ✓

screens.title: Graded vs complementary — well done!
screens.body: How cool is this? You now know something that most grown-ups don't — there are actually two types of antonym:

steps.why: A scale with steps in between

steps.text: Both are valid antonyms — knowing the type helps you pick!

learningGoal: Why picking a DIFFERENT word is the most common antonym trap — knowing this gives you a huge advantage!
How to check if a word is truly opposite or just unrelated

screens.title: Is "poor" really the opposite of "generous"?
screens.body:
Lily is spotting the most common antonym mistake. Someone picked **"poor"** as the opposite of **"generous"** — because generous people give money and poor people don't have money.

This is the **number one trap** in the whole 11+ antonym section — and the brilliant news is, once you know about it, you'll never fall for it again!

steps.text: Answer chosen: "poor" — is this REALLY the opposite?

screens.title: Same quality, opposite end!
screens.body:
'Poor' means having little money — but that's not the opposite of generous. A poor person can still be generous!

This is the golden rule: an antonym must flip the **SAME quality**. If the original word is about generosity, the opposite must also be about generosity — not about something else entirely!

steps.why: 'Poor' means having little money — but that's not the opposite of generous. A poor person can still be generous!
'Selfish' means unwilling to share — the exact opposite of 'generous' (willing to give freely). 'Poor' is about wealth, not character.

screens.title: Find the TRUE opposite!
screens.body:
Which word is the **true opposite** of **"polite"**?

Remember: it must flip the SAME quality!

interaction.question: Which is the true antonym of "polite"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Rude' is the true opposite of 'polite' — both describe manners. 'Shy' describes personality, not behaviour towards others. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "rude". 'Rude' is the true opposite of 'polite' — both describe manners. 'Shy' describes personality, not behaviour towards others.

screens.title: The golden rule of antonyms
screens.body: This is one of the most useful things you'll learn for the 11+. Remember this one rule and you'll ace antonym questions every time:

steps.text: 1. What quality does the word describe?
2. The antonym must describe THAT SAME quality
3. A DIFFERENT quality is NOT an antonym

learningGoal: How to find opposite emotions at the same intensity level — like turning a dial, not smashing a button!
Why matching the intensity matters for emotion antonyms

screens.title: What's the opposite of feeling "brave"?
screens.body:
Daisy is matching opposite emotions.

**"brave"** means having courage and not showing fear.

Here's the thing about feelings — the opposite must flip the emotion **at the same level of intensity**. Think of it like a seesaw — both sides need to be the same height for it to balance!

visualProps.relationship: having courage and not showing fear (medium-strong)
visualProps.label: What is the OPPOSITE feeling?

screens.title: Flip the feeling, match the level
screens.body: **"brave"** means having courage and not showing fear. The opposite emotion must be about the **same thing** (e.g. courage, patience, happiness) and at the **same intensity** (medium-strong).

steps.text: "brave" = having courage and not showing fear
"cowardly" = lacking courage and giving in to fear

steps.why: 'Terrified' is an emotion (extreme fear), but 'brave' is a character trait — they're not the same type

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which word is the **opposite** of **"generous"**? Remember to match the intensity!

interaction.question: Which is an antonym of "generous"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Generous' means willing to share and 'selfish' means unwilling to share — they are direct opposites about the same quality at a similar intensity. 'Greedy' is about wanting more, not about refusing to share. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "selfish". 'Generous' means willing to share and 'selfish' means unwilling to share — they are direct opposites about the same quality at a similar intensity. 'Greedy' is about wanting more, not about refusing to share. ✓

visualProps.relationship: willing to give and share freely (moderate)
visualProps.label: Find the OPPOSITE feeling

screens.title: Opposite feelings — same strength
screens.body: Brilliant work! You've got a great eye for matching intensity. For emotion antonyms, keep this seesaw idea in mind:

steps.why: Both moderate — about having or lacking courage

learningGoal: How to recognise advanced antonym pairs that appear in the 11+ — these are the ones that separate good scores from great ones!
How to use elimination and word roots to work out unfamiliar pairs (you'll feel like a word detective)

screens.title: What's the opposite of "benevolent"?
screens.body:
Evie is tackling advanced antonym pairs.

Don't let **"benevolent"** intimidate you — it just means: well-meaning and kind

Here's a clue to get you going: 'Bene-' means good (like 'benefit') and 'male-' means bad (like 'malicious')

Can you work out which option is the true opposite? You've absolutely got this!

screens.title: Use roots and elimination!
screens.body: **"benevolent"** means well-meaning and kind. Even when the words look scary, you have two brilliant strategies: **word roots** (prefixes/suffixes that give clues) and **elimination** (crossing out what you KNOW is wrong).

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Define, flip, then match — that's the recipe. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first define the word, then flip its meaning, then find the match.

steps.text: Flip it: wishing harm on others; evil-intentioned
steps.why: What does the opposite look like?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which word is the **opposite** of **"transparent"**?

Hint: 'Trans-' means across/through — what means you CANNOT see through?

interaction.question: Which is an antonym of "transparent"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Transparent' means see-through and 'opaque' means impossible to see through — they are exact opposites. 'Dark' is about light levels, not visibility through a material. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "opaque". 'Transparent' means see-through and 'opaque' means impossible to see through — they are exact opposites. 'Dark' is about light levels, not visibility through a material. ✓

visualProps.relationship: easy to see through; clear and open
visualProps.label: Find the OPPOSITE

screens.title: Advanced antonyms — you're ready for these!
screens.body: Look at you tackling the tough ones! When you come across hard vocabulary in the exam, you now have a proper game plan:

steps.why: bene- = good, male- = bad, temp- = time

steps.text: 2. Define the word, then flip it
3. Eliminate options that are DIFFERENT, not opposite

learningGoal: How to find opposites for describing words (adjectives) — think about what quality is being described
Why checking WHAT is being described helps you pick the right antonym every time

screens.title: What's the opposite of "rough"?
screens.body:
Daisy is matching opposite describing words.

**"The wall had a rough surface."**

Here's the key question: what **quality** is **"rough"** describing about a surface? It's the **texture**. To find the opposite, you need a word that flips THAT exact quality on its head.

screens.title: Same quality, opposite direction
screens.body: **"rough"** describes the **texture** of a surface. An adjective antonym must describe the **same quality** but in the **opposite direction**.

steps.why: Same quality, flipped to the other end

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
**"They walked along a narrow path through the woods."**

Which word is the **opposite** of **"narrow"**?

interaction.question: Which is an antonym of "narrow"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Narrow' and 'wide' are opposites describing WIDTH. 'Long' describes length, which is a completely different measurement. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "wide". 'Narrow' and 'wide' are opposites describing WIDTH. 'Long' describes length, which is a completely different measurement. ✓

screens.title: Describing word antonyms — great work!
screens.body: You're getting really good at this! The trick with describing words is always the same — find the quality, then flip it. Here's your recipe:

steps.text: 1. What quality is the word describing?
2. Find the option that flips THAT quality
3. Reject words that describe different qualities
